texts.label: text
texts.orig: Upon completion, SORT displays the following message on the screen: 'Sorting completed'
texts.text: Upon completion, SORT displays the following message on the screen: 'Sorting completed'

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To halt or terminate the test before the completion message is displayed, press CONTROL-C on the keyboard.
texts.text: To halt or terminate the test before the completion message is displayed, press CONTROL-C on the keyboard.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A test procedure is often called a test script because it gives us a step-by-step description of how to perform the test. A rigidly defined set of steps gives us control over the test, so that we can duplicate conditions and re-create the failure, if necessary, when trying to find the cause of the problem. If the test is interrupted for some reason, we must be able to continue the test without having to return to the beginning.
texts.text: A test procedure is often called a test script because it gives us a step-by-step description of how to perform the test. A rigidly defined set of steps gives us control over the test, so that we can duplicate conditions and re-create the failure, if necessary, when trying to find the cause of the problem. If the test is interrupted for some reason, we must be able to continue the test without having to return to the beginning.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The test script steps are numbered, and data associated with each step are referenced. If we have not described them elsewhere, we explain how to prepare the data or the site for the test. For example, the equipment settings needed, the database definitions, and the communication connections may be detailed. Next, the script explains exactly what is to happen during the test. We report the keys pressed, the screens displayed, the output produced, the equipment reactions, and any other manifestation.We explain the expected outcome or output, and we give instructions to the operator or user about what to do if the expected outcome is different from the actual outcome.
texts.text: The test script steps are numbered, and data associated with each step are referenced. If we have not described them elsewhere, we explain how to prepare the data or the site for the test. For example, the equipment settings needed, the database definitions, and the communication connections may be detailed. Next, the script explains exactly what is to happen during the test. We report the keys pressed, the screens displayed, the output produced, the equipment reactions, and any other manifestation.We explain the expected outcome or output, and we give instructions to the operator or user about what to do if the expected outcome is different from the actual outcome.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Finally, the test description explains the sequence of activities required to end the test. These activities may involve reading or printing critical data, terminating automated procedures, or turning off pieces of equipment.
texts.text: Finally, the test description explains the sequence of activities required to end the test. These activities may involve reading or printing critical data, terminating automated procedures, or turning off pieces of equipment.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Test Analysis Report
texts.text: Test Analysis Report

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When a test has been administered, we analyze the results to determine if the function or performance tested meets the requirements. Sometimes, the mere demonstration of a function is enough. Most of the time, though, there are performance constraints on the function. For instance, it is not enough to know that a column can be sorted or summed.We must measure the calculation's speed and note its correctness.Thus, a test analysis report is necessary for several reasons:
texts.text: When a test has been administered, we analyze the results to determine if the function or performance tested meets the requirements. Sometimes, the mere demonstration of a function is enough. Most of the time, though, there are performance constraints on the function. For instance, it is not enough to know that a column can be sorted or summed.We must measure the calculation's speed and note its correctness.Thus, a test analysis report is necessary for several reasons:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · It documents the results of a test.
texts.text: It documents the results of a test.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · If a failure occurs, the report provides information needed to duplicate the failure (if necessary) and to locate and fix the source of the problem.
texts.text: If a failure occurs, the report provides information needed to duplicate the failure (if necessary) and to locate and fix the source of the problem.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · It  provides  information  necessary  to  determine  if  the  development  project  is complete.
texts.text: It  provides  information  necessary  to  determine  if  the  development  project  is complete.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · It establishes confidence in the system's performance.
texts.text: It establishes confidence in the system's performance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The test analysis report may be read by people who were not part of the test process but who are familiar with other aspects of the system and its development.Thus, the report includes a brief summary of the project, its objectives, and relevant references for this test. For example, the test report mentions those parts of the requirements, design,
texts.text: The test analysis report may be read by people who were not part of the test process but who are familiar with other aspects of the system and its development.Thus, the report includes a brief summary of the project, its objectives, and relevant references for this test. For example, the test report mentions those parts of the requirements, design,

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Chapter 9
texts.text: Chapter 9

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Testing the System
texts.text: Testing the System

texts.label: text
texts.orig: and implementation documents that describe the functions exercised in this test. The report also indicates those parts of the test plan and specification documents that deal with this test.
texts.text: and implementation documents that describe the functions exercised in this test. The report also indicates those parts of the test plan and specification documents that deal with this test.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Once the stage is set in this way, the test analysis report lists the functions and performance characteristics that were to be demonstrated and describes the actual results. The results include function, performance, and data measures, noting whether the target requirements have been met. If a fault or deficiency has been discovered, the report discusses its impact. Sometimes, we evaluate the test results in terms of a measure of severity. This measure helps the test team decide whether to continue testing or wait until the fault has been corrected. For example, if the failure is a spurious character in the upper part of a display screen, then testing can continue while we locate the cause and correct it. However, if a fault causes the system to crash or a data file to be deleted, the test team may decide to interrupt testing until the fault is repaired.
texts.text: Once the stage is set in this way, the test analysis report lists the functions and performance characteristics that were to be demonstrated and describes the actual results. The results include function, performance, and data measures, noting whether the target requirements have been met. If a fault or deficiency has been discovered, the report discusses its impact. Sometimes, we evaluate the test results in terms of a measure of severity. This measure helps the test team decide whether to continue testing or wait until the fault has been corrected. For example, if the failure is a spurious character in the upper part of a display screen, then testing can continue while we locate the cause and correct it. However, if a fault causes the system to crash or a data file to be deleted, the test team may decide to interrupt testing until the fault is repaired.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Problem Report Forms
texts.text: Problem Report Forms

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recall from Chapter 1 that a fault is a problem in a system artifact that can cause a system failure; the fault is seen by the developer, and the failure is experienced by the user. During  testing, we  capture  data  about  faults  and  failures  in problem  report  forms . A discrepancy report form is a problem report that describes occurrences of problems where  actual  system  behaviors  or  attributes  do  not  match  with  what  we  expect. It explains what was expected, what actually happened, and the circumstances leading to the  failure. A fault  report  form explains  how  a  fault  was  found  and  fixed, often  in response to the filing of a discrepancy report form.
texts.text: Recall from Chapter 1 that a fault is a problem in a system artifact that can cause a system failure; the fault is seen by the developer, and the failure is experienced by the user. During  testing, we  capture  data  about  faults  and  failures  in problem  report  forms . A discrepancy report form is a problem report that describes occurrences of problems where  actual  system  behaviors  or  attributes  do  not  match  with  what  we  expect. It explains what was expected, what actually happened, and the circumstances leading to the  failure. A fault  report  form explains  how  a  fault  was  found  and  fixed, often  in response to the filing of a discrepancy report form.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Every problem report form should answer several questions about the problem it is describing:
texts.text: Every problem report form should answer several questions about the problem it is describing:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Location: Where did the problem occur?
texts.text: Location: Where did the problem occur?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Timing: When did it occur?
texts.text: Timing: When did it occur?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Symptom: What was observed?
texts.text: Symptom: What was observed?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · End-result: What were the consequences?
texts.text: End-result: What were the consequences?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Mechanism: How did it occur?
texts.text: Mechanism: How did it occur?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Cause: Why did it occur?
texts.text: Cause: Why did it occur?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Severity: How much was the user or business affected?
texts.text: Severity: How much was the user or business affected?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Cost: How much did it cost?
texts.text: Cost: How much did it cost?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 9.12 is an example of an actual fault report form from a British utility. Notice that a fault number is assigned to each fault, and the developers record the date when they were notified that a problem had occurred as well as the date when the
texts.text: Figure 9.12 is an example of an actual fault report form from a British utility. Notice that a fault number is assigned to each fault, and the developers record the date when they were notified that a problem had occurred as well as the date when the

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Test Documentation
texts.text: Test Documentation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: problem's cause was located and fixed. Because developers do not address each problem right away (e.g., because other problems have higher priority), the developers also record the actual number of hours needed to repair this particular fault.
texts.text: problem's cause was located and fixed. Because developers do not address each problem right away (e.g., because other problems have higher priority), the developers also record the actual number of hours needed to repair this particular fault.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, the fault report form is missing a great deal of data. In general, a fault report form should address our list of questions with this kind of detailed information (Fenton and Pfleeger 1997):
texts.text: However, the fault report form is missing a great deal of data. In general, a fault report form should address our list of questions with this kind of detailed information (Fenton and Pfleeger 1997):

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Location: within-system identifier, such as module or document name
texts.text: Location: within-system identifier, such as module or document name

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Timing: phases  of  development  during  which  fault  was  created, detected, and corrected
texts.text: Timing: phases  of  development  during  which  fault  was  created, detected, and corrected

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Symptom: type of error message reported or activity that revealed fault (e.g., testing, review, inspection)
texts.text: Symptom: type of error message reported or activity that revealed fault (e.g., testing, review, inspection)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · End-result: failure caused by the fault
texts.text: End-result: failure caused by the fault

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Mechanism: how the source was created, detected, and corrected
texts.text: Mechanism: how the source was created, detected, and corrected

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Cause: type of human error that led to the fault
texts.text: Cause: type of human error that led to the fault

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Severity: refers to severity of resulting or potential failure
texts.text: Severity: refers to severity of resulting or potential failure

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Cost: time or effort to locate and correct; can include analysis of cost had fault been identified earlier in the development
texts.text: Cost: time or effort to locate and correct; can include analysis of cost had fault been identified earlier in the development

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Notice that each of these aspects of a fault reflects the developer's understanding of the fault's impact on the system. On the other hand, a discrepancy report should reflect a user's view of the failure caused by a fault.The questions are the same, but the answers are very different:
texts.text: Notice that each of these aspects of a fault reflects the developer's understanding of the fault's impact on the system. On the other hand, a discrepancy report should reflect a user's view of the failure caused by a fault.The questions are the same, but the answers are very different:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Location: installation where the failure was observed
texts.text: Location: installation where the failure was observed

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Timing: CPU time, clock time, or other relevant measure of time
texts.text: Timing: CPU time, clock time, or other relevant measure of time

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Symptom: type of error message or indication of failure
texts.text: Symptom: type of error message or indication of failure

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · End-result: description  of  failure, such  as  'operating  system  crash,' 'service degraded,''loss of data,''wrong output,' or 'no output'
texts.text: End-result: description  of  failure, such  as  'operating  system  crash,' 'service degraded,''loss of data,''wrong output,' or 'no output'

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Mechanism: chain of events, including keyboard commands and state data leading to failure
texts.text: Mechanism: chain of events, including keyboard commands and state data leading to failure

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Cause: reference to possible faults leading to failure
texts.text: Cause: reference to possible faults leading to failure

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Severity: impact on user or business
texts.text: Severity: impact on user or business

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Cost: cost to fix plus cost of lost potential business
texts.text: Cost: cost to fix plus cost of lost potential business

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure  9.13 is  an  actual  discrepancy  report  form, mistakenly  called  a  'fault' report. It addresses many of the questions in our list and does a good job of describing the failure. However, it contains a list only of the items changed, not a description of the underlying cause of the failure. Ideally, this form should reference one or more fault reports, so that we can tell which faults caused which failures.
texts.text: Figure  9.13 is  an  actual  discrepancy  report  form, mistakenly  called  a  'fault' report. It addresses many of the questions in our list and does a good job of describing the failure. However, it contains a list only of the items changed, not a description of the underlying cause of the failure. Ideally, this form should reference one or more fault reports, so that we can tell which faults caused which failures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We need more complete information in our problem report forms so we can evaluate the effectiveness and efficiency of our testing and development practices. Especially  when  we  have  limited  resources, historical  information  captured  in  problem reports helps us to understand which activities are likely to cause faults, and which practices are good at finding and fixing them.
texts.text: We need more complete information in our problem report forms so we can evaluate the effectiveness and efficiency of our testing and development practices. Especially  when  we  have  limited  resources, historical  information  captured  in  problem reports helps us to understand which activities are likely to cause faults, and which practices are good at finding and fixing them.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 9.9 TESTING SAFETY-CRITICAL SYSTEMS
texts.text: 9.9 TESTING SAFETY-CRITICAL SYSTEMS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Chapter 1, we looked at several examples of systems whose failure can harm or kill people. Such systems are called safety-critical , since the consequences of their failure are so severe.Anthes (1997) reported many other instances where software failures led to unacceptable levels of harm. For example, from 1986 to 1996, 450 reports were filed with  the  U.S. Food  and  Drug Administration, describing  software  faults  in  medical devices. And 24 of these reports involved software that led to death or injury. Among the problems reported were these:
texts.text: In Chapter 1, we looked at several examples of systems whose failure can harm or kill people. Such systems are called safety-critical , since the consequences of their failure are so severe.Anthes (1997) reported many other instances where software failures led to unacceptable levels of harm. For example, from 1986 to 1996, 450 reports were filed with  the  U.S. Food  and  Drug Administration, describing  software  faults  in  medical devices. And 24 of these reports involved software that led to death or injury. Among the problems reported were these:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · An intravenous medication pump ran dry and injected air into a patient.
texts.text: An intravenous medication pump ran dry and injected air into a patient.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · A monitor failed to sound an alarm when a patient's heart stopped beating.
texts.text: A monitor failed to sound an alarm when a patient's heart stopped beating.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · A respirator delivered 'unscheduled breaths' to a patient.
texts.text: A respirator delivered 'unscheduled breaths' to a patient.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · A  digital  display  combined  the  name  of  one  patient  with  medical  data  from another patient.
texts.text: A  digital  display  combined  the  name  of  one  patient  with  medical  data  from another patient.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The problems are not necessarily indicative of declining software quality. Rather, they reflect the increasing amount of software being placed in safety-critical systems.
texts.text: The problems are not necessarily indicative of declining software quality. Rather, they reflect the increasing amount of software being placed in safety-critical systems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Unfortunately, we do not always understand how the software development process affects the characteristics of the products we build, so it is difficult for us to ensure
texts.text: Unfortunately, we do not always understand how the software development process affects the characteristics of the products we build, so it is difficult for us to ensure

texts.orig: that safety-critical systems are safe enough. In particular, we do not know how much each practice or technique contributes to a product's reliability. At the same time, our customers require us to reach ever-higher levels of ultra-high reliability.
texts.text: that safety-critical systems are safe enough. In particular, we do not know how much each practice or technique contributes to a product's reliability. At the same time, our customers require us to reach ever-higher levels of ultra-high reliability.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For  instance, the  Airbus  320  is  a  fly-by-wire  aircraft, meaning  that  software controls most of its vital functions. Because the airplane cannot tolerate failure of its fly-by-wire software, its system reliability requirement is a failure rate of per hour (Rouquet and Traverse 1986).The software requirement must be even more restrictive. 10 -9
texts.text: For  instance, the  Airbus  320  is  a  fly-by-wire  aircraft, meaning  that  software controls most of its vital functions. Because the airplane cannot tolerate failure of its fly-by-wire software, its system reliability requirement is a failure rate of per hour (Rouquet and Traverse 1986).The software requirement must be even more restrictive. 10 -9

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The prescribed failure rate means that the system can tolerate at most one failure in hours. In other words, the system can fail at most once in over 100,000 years of operation.We say that a system has ultra-high reliability when it has at most one failure in hours. It is clear that we cannot apply our usual reliability assessment techniques in cases like this; to do so would mean running the system for 100,000 years (at least) and tracking the failure behavior.Thus, we must seek other practices to help assure the software's reliability. 10 9 10 9
texts.text: The prescribed failure rate means that the system can tolerate at most one failure in hours. In other words, the system can fail at most once in over 100,000 years of operation.We say that a system has ultra-high reliability when it has at most one failure in hours. It is clear that we cannot apply our usual reliability assessment techniques in cases like this; to do so would mean running the system for 100,000 years (at least) and tracking the failure behavior.Thus, we must seek other practices to help assure the software's reliability. 10 9 10 9

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 9.14 shows us another way to look at the ultra-high-reliability problem.We have graphed failure data from a system in operational use; software and hardware design changes were implemented to fix the causes of the failures. In the graph, the Littlewood-Verrall  model  was  used  to  compute  the  current  Rate  of  Occurrence  of Failures (ROCOF) at various times as testing proceeds. The dashed line, fitted manually, shows an apparently clear law of diminishing returns because the slope of the line flattens out, failures occur less and less frequently. That is, we must test for longer periods of time to make the system fail again, so reliability is increasing.
texts.text: Figure 9.14 shows us another way to look at the ultra-high-reliability problem.We have graphed failure data from a system in operational use; software and hardware design changes were implemented to fix the causes of the failures. In the graph, the Littlewood-Verrall  model  was  used  to  compute  the  current  Rate  of  Occurrence  of Failures (ROCOF) at various times as testing proceeds. The dashed line, fitted manually, shows an apparently clear law of diminishing returns because the slope of the line flattens out, failures occur less and less frequently. That is, we must test for longer periods of time to make the system fail again, so reliability is increasing.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However,it is not at all clear what the ultimate reliability will be.We cannot tell if the curve is asymptotic to zero or whether we reach a nonzero reliability because we are introducing new faults as we fix old ones. Even if we felt confident that the system could reach ultra-high levels of reliability, we would still need to test for extraordinarily long periods of time to demonstrate our confidence.
texts.text: However,it is not at all clear what the ultimate reliability will be.We cannot tell if the curve is asymptotic to zero or whether we reach a nonzero reliability because we are introducing new faults as we fix old ones. Even if we felt confident that the system could reach ultra-high levels of reliability, we would still need to test for extraordinarily long periods of time to demonstrate our confidence.

texts.text: Testing the System

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In fact, even when we test a program for a long time without a failure, we still do not have the level of assurance we need. Littlewood has shown that if a program has worked failure-free for x hours, there is about a 50:50 chance that it will survive the next x hours before failing. To have the kind of confidence apparently needed for an aircraft such as the A320 would require a failure-free performance of the software for several billion hours (Littlewood 1991). Thus, even if the system had actually achieved its target reliability, we could not assure ourselves of it in an acceptable period of time.
texts.text: In fact, even when we test a program for a long time without a failure, we still do not have the level of assurance we need. Littlewood has shown that if a program has worked failure-free for x hours, there is about a 50:50 chance that it will survive the next x hours before failing. To have the kind of confidence apparently needed for an aircraft such as the A320 would require a failure-free performance of the software for several billion hours (Littlewood 1991). Thus, even if the system had actually achieved its target reliability, we could not assure ourselves of it in an acceptable period of time.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ensuring very high levels of reliability is a difficult but critical challenge that must be met if we hope to continue to use software in safety-critical systems. Many software engineers suggest that we should use formal verification techniques with our requirements, designs, and code. But formal evaluation of natural language is impossible, and important information may be lost if we translate natural language to mathematical symbols. Even formal proofs of specification and design are not foolproof, because mistakes are sometimes made in the proofs. For this reason, companies such as Baltimore Gas and Electric (Sidebar 9.9) rely on a combination of quality assurance techniques to try to catch or prevent as many faults as possible. Sidebar 9.10 lists additional industrial approaches to increasing safety. At the same time, researchers have been looking for other methods to help developers understand and ensure reliability. We will look at three of these techniques: design diversity, software safety cases, and cleanroom.
texts.text: Ensuring very high levels of reliability is a difficult but critical challenge that must be met if we hope to continue to use software in safety-critical systems. Many software engineers suggest that we should use formal verification techniques with our requirements, designs, and code. But formal evaluation of natural language is impossible, and important information may be lost if we translate natural language to mathematical symbols. Even formal proofs of specification and design are not foolproof, because mistakes are sometimes made in the proofs. For this reason, companies such as Baltimore Gas and Electric (Sidebar 9.9) rely on a combination of quality assurance techniques to try to catch or prevent as many faults as possible. Sidebar 9.10 lists additional industrial approaches to increasing safety. At the same time, researchers have been looking for other methods to help developers understand and ensure reliability. We will look at three of these techniques: design diversity, software safety cases, and cleanroom.

texts.text: Design Diversity

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Design  diversity, introduced  in  Chapter  5, is  based  on  a  simple  philosophy. The same system is built according to the same requirements specification but in several
texts.text: Design  diversity, introduced  in  Chapter  5, is  based  on  a  simple  philosophy. The same system is built according to the same requirements specification but in several

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: SIDEBAR 9.9 SOFTWARE QUALITY PRACTICES AT BALTIMORE GAS AND ELECTRIC
texts.text: SIDEBAR 9.9 SOFTWARE QUALITY PRACTICES AT BALTIMORE GAS AND ELECTRIC

texts.label: text
texts.orig: I n Maryland, the Baltimore Gas and Electric Company (BG&E) uses no special tools or techniques when it develops the safety-critical software that controls its two nuclear reactors. However, managers hope to ensure high reliability by checking the requirements definition thoroughly, performing quality reviews, testing carefully, documenting completely, and performing thorough configuration control.
texts.text: I n Maryland, the Baltimore Gas and Electric Company (BG&E) uses no special tools or techniques when it develops the safety-critical software that controls its two nuclear reactors. However, managers hope to ensure high reliability by checking the requirements definition thoroughly, performing quality reviews, testing carefully, documenting completely, and performing thorough configuration control.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To make sure all problems are caught early, the system is reviewed twice. Both the information systems group and the nuclear design engineering group conduct design reviews, code reviews, and system tests.
texts.text: To make sure all problems are caught early, the system is reviewed twice. Both the information systems group and the nuclear design engineering group conduct design reviews, code reviews, and system tests.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  United  States  government  has  issued  federal  Quality  Assurance  Criteria  for Nuclear Power Plants, and software from BG&E's internal information systems group must comply with these regulations. Moreover, when a vendor supplies software to be used as part of the control system, BG&E sends an audit team to the vendor's development site, to ensure that the vendor has a software quality assurance program that meets the regulations (Anthes 1997).
texts.text: The  United  States  government  has  issued  federal  Quality  Assurance  Criteria  for Nuclear Power Plants, and software from BG&E's internal information systems group must comply with these regulations. Moreover, when a vendor supplies software to be used as part of the control system, BG&E sends an audit team to the vendor's development site, to ensure that the vendor has a software quality assurance program that meets the regulations (Anthes 1997).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: SIDEBAR 9.10 SUGGESTIONS FOR BUILDING SAFETY-CRITICAL SOFTWARE
texts.text: SIDEBAR 9.10 SUGGESTIONS FOR BUILDING SAFETY-CRITICAL SOFTWARE

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A nthes (1997) suggests several steps for building and testing safety-critical systems, as proposed by industry consultants:
texts.text: A nthes (1997) suggests several steps for building and testing safety-critical systems, as proposed by industry consultants:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Recognize that testing cannot remove all faults or risks.
texts.text: Recognize that testing cannot remove all faults or risks.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Do not confuse safety, reliability, and security. A system that is 100% reliable still may be neither secure nor safe.
texts.text: Do not confuse safety, reliability, and security. A system that is 100% reliable still may be neither secure nor safe.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Tightly link your organization's software and safety organizations.
texts.text: Tightly link your organization's software and safety organizations.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Build and use a safety information system.
texts.text: Build and use a safety information system.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Instill a management culture of safety.
texts.text: Instill a management culture of safety.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Assume that every mistake users can make will be made.
texts.text: Assume that every mistake users can make will be made.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Do not assume that low-probability, high-impact events will not happen.
texts.text: Do not assume that low-probability, high-impact events will not happen.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Emphasize requirements definition, testing, code and specification reviews, and configuration control.
texts.text: Emphasize requirements definition, testing, code and specification reviews, and configuration control.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Do not let short-term cost considerations overshadow long-term risks and costs.
texts.text: Do not let short-term cost considerations overshadow long-term risks and costs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: independent ways, each according to a different design. Each system runs in parallel with the others, and a voting scheme coordinates actions when one system's results differ from the others'. The underlying assumption is that it is unlikely that at least three of the five groups of developers will write incorrect software for a given requirement, so  high  reliability  is  likely  (Avizienis  and  Kelly  1984). Several  systems  have been built using this technique, including the software for the U.S. space shuttle and the Airbus A320 (Rouquet and Traverse 1986). However, there is empirical evidence suggesting that independently developed software versions will not fail independently; the diverse designs do not always offer reliability higher than that of a single version.
texts.text: independent ways, each according to a different design. Each system runs in parallel with the others, and a voting scheme coordinates actions when one system's results differ from the others'. The underlying assumption is that it is unlikely that at least three of the five groups of developers will write incorrect software for a given requirement, so  high  reliability  is  likely  (Avizienis  and  Kelly  1984). Several  systems  have been built using this technique, including the software for the U.S. space shuttle and the Airbus A320 (Rouquet and Traverse 1986). However, there is empirical evidence suggesting that independently developed software versions will not fail independently; the diverse designs do not always offer reliability higher than that of a single version.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For example, Knight and Leveson (1986) performed an experiment in which 27 versions of a software system were developed independently.They examined the faults discovered in each system and found a high incidence of common ones. Knight and Leveson speculate that, because we train our software developers to use common techniques and common approaches to design, we can expect different designers and developers  to  make  the  same  kinds  of  mistakes. Eckhardt  and  Lee  (1985)  discuss  a theoretical scenario, based on the notion of varying the difficulty of different inputs, that supports these empirical findings.
texts.text: For example, Knight and Leveson (1986) performed an experiment in which 27 versions of a software system were developed independently.They examined the faults discovered in each system and found a high incidence of common ones. Knight and Leveson speculate that, because we train our software developers to use common techniques and common approaches to design, we can expect different designers and developers  to  make  the  same  kinds  of  mistakes. Eckhardt  and  Lee  (1985)  discuss  a theoretical scenario, based on the notion of varying the difficulty of different inputs, that supports these empirical findings.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Miller (1986) points out that, even if we build redundant systems that fail independently, we must still try to estimate the dependence between any two versions. He shows that this demonstration is as difficult as the problem of testing the resulting system as a black box, and claims this to be an essentially impossible task.
texts.text: Miller (1986) points out that, even if we build redundant systems that fail independently, we must still try to estimate the dependence between any two versions. He shows that this demonstration is as difficult as the problem of testing the resulting system as a black box, and claims this to be an essentially impossible task.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Software Safety Cases
texts.text: Software Safety Cases

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Testing  is  necessarily  connected  to  software's  design  and  implementation. We  can examine the design to help us define test cases, as well as to determine when we have considered  all  possible  scenarios. Fenelon  et  al. (1994)  suggest  that  we  look  at  the quality  of  safety-critical  systems  by  listing  the  system's  goals, investigating  how  the design meets those goals, and ensuring that the implementation matches the design. Overall, we  want  the  system  to  be safe , that  is, free  from  accident  or  loss. We  can decompose the safety goals and assign failure rates or constraints to each component of the design, so that satisfying each lower-level goal will 'roll up' to allow us to meet safety goals for the entire system. In this way, we make a safety case for the system, making explicit the ways in which our software meets performance goals for safetycritical systems.
texts.text: Testing  is  necessarily  connected  to  software's  design  and  implementation. We  can examine the design to help us define test cases, as well as to determine when we have considered  all  possible  scenarios. Fenelon  et  al. (1994)  suggest  that  we  look  at  the quality  of  safety-critical  systems  by  listing  the  system's  goals, investigating  how  the design meets those goals, and ensuring that the implementation matches the design. Overall, we  want  the  system  to  be safe , that  is, free  from  accident  or  loss. We  can decompose the safety goals and assign failure rates or constraints to each component of the design, so that satisfying each lower-level goal will 'roll up' to allow us to meet safety goals for the entire system. In this way, we make a safety case for the system, making explicit the ways in which our software meets performance goals for safetycritical systems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We can analyze a system from four different perspectives: knowing the cause or not, and knowing the effects or not. In each instance, we want to establish links between situations that lead to normal behavior and those that lead to potential failure. Table 9.6 illustrates steps we can take in each case. Used during design, these analyses help us plan ways to avoid failure; used during testing, they help us identify important test cases.
texts.text: We can analyze a system from four different perspectives: knowing the cause or not, and knowing the effects or not. In each instance, we want to establish links between situations that lead to normal behavior and those that lead to potential failure. Table 9.6 illustrates steps we can take in each case. Used during design, these analyses help us plan ways to avoid failure; used during testing, they help us identify important test cases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We saw in Chapter 5 how fault-tree analysis allows us to examine possible effects and trace them back to their likely root causes. Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA) complements fault-tree analysis by working from known failure modes to unknown system effects. We say that a hazard is a system state that, together with the right conditions, will lead to an accident.A failure mode is a situation that can give rise to a hazard. For example, the overflow in the Ariane-4 SRI is a hazard; it did not cause Ariane-4 to fail, because the associated conditions did not occur (but they certainly did on Ariane-5). The failure mode for Ariane-5 is the situation where the SRI ran longer than the period for which it was designed.
texts.text: We saw in Chapter 5 how fault-tree analysis allows us to examine possible effects and trace them back to their likely root causes. Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA) complements fault-tree analysis by working from known failure modes to unknown system effects. We say that a hazard is a system state that, together with the right conditions, will lead to an accident.A failure mode is a situation that can give rise to a hazard. For example, the overflow in the Ariane-4 SRI is a hazard; it did not cause Ariane-4 to fail, because the associated conditions did not occur (but they certainly did on Ariane-5). The failure mode for Ariane-5 is the situation where the SRI ran longer than the period for which it was designed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: FMEA is highly labor-intensive and based on the experience of the analysts. It usually involves our performing an initial analysis of the software design, abstracting modes that might lead to failures. Then we look at how combinations of the basic failure modes might lead to actual failures. Sidebar 9.11 describes how FMEA, in concert with other techniques, might have improved the safety of Therac-25.
texts.text: FMEA is highly labor-intensive and based on the experience of the analysts. It usually involves our performing an initial analysis of the software design, abstracting modes that might lead to failures. Then we look at how combinations of the basic failure modes might lead to actual failures. Sidebar 9.11 describes how FMEA, in concert with other techniques, might have improved the safety of Therac-25.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hazard  and  operability  studies  (HAZOPS) involve  a  structured  analysis  to anticipate system hazards and to suggest means to avoid or deal with them. They are based on a technique developed by the Imperial Chemical Industries (UK) in the 1960s
texts.text: Hazard  and  operability  studies  (HAZOPS) involve  a  structured  analysis  to anticipate system hazards and to suggest means to avoid or deal with them. They are based on a technique developed by the Imperial Chemical Industries (UK) in the 1960s

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: SIDEBAR 9.11 SAFETY AND THE THERAC-25
texts.text: SIDEBAR 9.11 SAFETY AND THE THERAC-25

texts.label: text
texts.orig: B etween June 1985 and January 1987, a radiation therapy machine known as the Therac-25 was involved in six known accidents, causing death and serious injury resulting from massive overdoses. Leveson and Turner (1993) describe the machine, the accidents, and the software  issues  in  great  detail, and  their  article  should  be  required  reading  for  systems  and software engineers who design and build safety-critical systems.
texts.text: B etween June 1985 and January 1987, a radiation therapy machine known as the Therac-25 was involved in six known accidents, causing death and serious injury resulting from massive overdoses. Leveson and Turner (1993) describe the machine, the accidents, and the software  issues  in  great  detail, and  their  article  should  be  required  reading  for  systems  and software engineers who design and build safety-critical systems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  software  was  written  by  a  single  person, using  PDP-11  assembly  language  and reusing code from an earlier machine called the Therac-6. Some of the software was tested on a simulator, but most of it was tested as part of the larger system, using mostly integrated system tests (i.e., there was minimal unit and software testing).
texts.text: The  software  was  written  by  a  single  person, using  PDP-11  assembly  language  and reusing code from an earlier machine called the Therac-6. Some of the software was tested on a simulator, but most of it was tested as part of the larger system, using mostly integrated system tests (i.e., there was minimal unit and software testing).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Atomic Energy of Canada Limited (AECL) performed a safety analysis of the Therac25 system. AECL began with a failure modes and effects analysis to identify single failures leading to significant hazards. Then, to identify multiple failures and quantify the results, it performed a fault-tree analysis. Finally, it hired an outside consultant to perform detailed code inspections of the software functions related to the most serious hazards: electron-beam scanning, energy selection, beam shutoff, and dose calibration. The AECL final report recommended 10 changes to the Therac-25 hardware, including interlocks to back up software control of energy selection and electron-beam scanning.
texts.text: Atomic Energy of Canada Limited (AECL) performed a safety analysis of the Therac25 system. AECL began with a failure modes and effects analysis to identify single failures leading to significant hazards. Then, to identify multiple failures and quantify the results, it performed a fault-tree analysis. Finally, it hired an outside consultant to perform detailed code inspections of the software functions related to the most serious hazards: electron-beam scanning, energy selection, beam shutoff, and dose calibration. The AECL final report recommended 10 changes to the Therac-25 hardware, including interlocks to back up software control of energy selection and electron-beam scanning.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Leveson and Turner (1993) describe how the underlying cause of the problems was a timing error that was difficult to reproduce. They point out that most computer-related accidents result from requirements faults, not from coding faults, and they list several basic software engineering principles that were violated by the Therac-25:
texts.text: Leveson and Turner (1993) describe how the underlying cause of the problems was a timing error that was difficult to reproduce. They point out that most computer-related accidents result from requirements faults, not from coding faults, and they list several basic software engineering principles that were violated by the Therac-25:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Documentation should be done as development progresses, not afterward.
texts.text: Documentation should be done as development progresses, not afterward.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Software quality assurance practices should be an integral part of the development process. These practices should include standards that are set early and used to evaluate intermediate products.
texts.text: Software quality assurance practices should be an integral part of the development process. These practices should include standards that are set early and used to evaluate intermediate products.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Simple designs are easier to understand, code, and test than complex ones.
texts.text: Simple designs are easier to understand, code, and test than complex ones.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Software should be designed to anticipate failures and capture information about them.
texts.text: Software should be designed to anticipate failures and capture information about them.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · It is not enough to assume that system testing will catch software problems. Software should be tested extensively, as well as subjected to formal analysis, at the component and system levels before integration with the hardware.
texts.text: It is not enough to assume that system testing will catch software problems. Software should be tested extensively, as well as subjected to formal analysis, at the component and system levels before integration with the hardware.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: to analyze the design of a new chemical plant. HAZOPS uses guide words as part of an extensive  review  process, in  conjunction  with  an  analysis  of  control  and  data  flows between processing components,to help analysts identify hazards. Table 9.7 presents an example of guide words for a system where event timing, controlled by data and signals, is important for task coordination.
texts.text: to analyze the design of a new chemical plant. HAZOPS uses guide words as part of an extensive  review  process, in  conjunction  with  an  analysis  of  control  and  data  flows between processing components,to help analysts identify hazards. Table 9.7 presents an example of guide words for a system where event timing, controlled by data and signals, is important for task coordination.

Exploratory analysis, including hazard and operability studies
